original_hypothesis.title: Owner Can Toggle Ready State and Fragment Staking Liquidity
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `setReady` function in `BlastOnboardingBoot` allows the owner to toggle the `ready` state back to `false` after users have already claimed shares. The owner can then use `setStaking` to change the staking contract and set `ready` back to `true`. Early claimers will have their shares locked in the old staking contract, while new claimers will use the new one. This fragments liquidity and may orphan early users from future reward distributions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: State transitions that should be one-way are reversible, allowing admins to disrupt the protocol's intended operation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check preventing ready from being set to false after claims

preconditions: Owner acts maliciously or makes an operational error

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reversible state transition for a one-time bootstrapping event.

operational_questions: Is there any legitimate reason to change the staking contract after claims have started?
How would early claimers migrate to the new staking contract?
validation_requirements: Call setReady(true), claim shares, call setReady(false), setStaking(newStaking), setReady(true)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users claim and stake in Contract A
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Owner sets ready=false, changes staking to Contract B, sets ready=true
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: New users claim and stake in Contract B. Contract A is abandoned.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Fragmentation of staking liquidity and orphaned users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires manual migration of users

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
